+ Contemporary romance: Darkroom Revelations — Score: 79.1
Prompt:
Darkroom Revelations Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of Dani, a 25-year-old assertive art student known for her bold style and take-charge attitude in relationships. For the past semester, she's been quietly fascinated by Elliot (he/him), a 19-year-old boy in her experimental photography class. Dani has been intrigued by his unconventional masculinity: killer thrift-shop-nu-goth style, soft demeanour and quiet intellect. When they're paired for a final project requiring late nights in the darkroom, Elliot tentatively shares that he's been questioning his gender identity and expression. Set during one of these intimate darkroom sessions where the red light creates a confessional atmosphere. Explore Dani's complex attraction—her usual assertiveness complicated by her desire to protect Elliot's vulnerability, her excitement at witnessing his self-discovery, and her confusion about what this means for her own identity as someone typically attracted to more defined gender presentations. The narrative should capture the tender awkwardness of their conversations, Dani's internal struggle between taking charge and wanting to give Elliot agency, and the unexpected chemistry that develops as both find themselves in uncharted territory. 1000 words.
Model Output:
### Darkroom Revelations The darkroom smelled like vinegar and secrets, the red safelight casting everything in a bloody glow that made the world feel like a confession booth. It was past midnight, the campus dead quiet except for the hum of the enlarger and the distant drip of the timer. Elliot and I had been paired for this final project—experimental photography, all about capturing the unseen, the blurred edges of reality. Perfect, really, because that's exactly what was happening between us. I'm Dani. Twenty-five, art student, the one who struts into class in leather skirts and combat boots, the girl who pins her partners to the wall and makes the rules. I've got a type: sharp jaws, broad shoulders, that raw, unapologetic masculinity that I can wrestle into submission. But Elliot? He's nineteen, all thrift-store nu-goth glory—ripped black fishnets under baggy cargos, a faded Bauhaus tee clinging to his slim frame, silver rings on delicate fingers, and eyeliner that smudges just right. Soft voice, quieter intellect that slips out in class critiques like a hidden blade. I've been watching him all semester, my gaze lingering a beat too long on the way he tilts his head when he thinks no one's looking. Tonight, we were developing contact sheets from our shoot: abandoned industrial lots at dusk, shadows twisting like questions. The trays bubbled with developer, and images ghosted up from the black—slow, inevitable revelations. Elliot stood across from me at the stainless-steel table, sleeves rolled up to his elbows, humming softly. Some Cure tune, low and melancholic, vibrating in the humid air. It was intimate, this space. No windows, just us and the chem bath's alchemy. "God, these are coming out gorgeous," I said, my voice huskier than I meant under the red light. I leaned over the tray, assertive as always, pointing at a frame where rusted beams fractured the sky. "See that? Your framing's killer. Makes it feel like the world's cracking open." He glanced up, cheeks flushing under the crimson haze—hard to tell if it was the light or him. "Yeah? I wasn't sure about the exposure." His eyes darted back to the bath, fingers twisting a ring. That soft demeanor of his, it pulled at me. Usually, I'd close the distance, take charge, see how far that blush went. But tonight felt different. Charged. We worked in tandem, the rhythm building: me agitating the trays, him timing the stops. Silence stretched, broken only by his humming. Then, tentative as a test strip in fixer, he spoke. "Dani... can I tell you something? It's been kinda eating at me." I froze, tongs hovering. My heart kicked up, that protective urge surging alongside the heat low in my belly. "Yeah. Spill." He exhaled, staring into the developer like it held answers. "I've been... questioning things. Like, gender stuff. Expression, mostly. I don't know if I'm just goth and weird, or if it's more. Like, sometimes I look in the mirror and think... what if?" His voice cracked on the last word, vulnerable, raw. He didn't meet my eyes. The room shrank. Under the red light, his features softened even more—full lips, high cheekbones, that lithe body that suddenly read... ambiguous. Beautiful. My usual assertiveness jammed up. Part of me wanted to grab him, pull him close, shield that fragility with my body. Kiss away the doubt. But another part—the one thrilled by his unraveling—wanted to watch, to witness this boy cracking open like one of our prints. And fuck, it confused me. I like my attractions clear-cut: men who know they're men, so I can dominate the lines. This? This blurred everything. Was I still attracted? Hell yes. More, even. But to what? Who? "Elliot," I said, stepping closer, voice steady despite the storm inside. I rested a hand on the table near his, not touching—giving him space, agency. It killed me. "That's huge. And brave as shit to say out loud. Here, of all places." I wanted to take charge, map his body with my hands, show him he was wanted exactly as he was questioning. But no. Not yet. "How long?" "Awhile. Since high school, maybe. Class helped—playing with light, shadows. Hiding and revealing." He hummed again, softer, nervous. The tune wrapped around us, pulling me in. Our arms brushed as we both reached for the tray. Static sparked—chemistry, unexpected, electric. I nodded, internal tug-of-war raging. Protect him: yes. Push him: tempting. But this vulnerability? It flipped my script. "I get it. Kinda. I've always been the one drawing lines—bold strokes, no hesitation. Attracted to guys who own their space. But you..." I trailed off, heat rising. Honesty spilled out, confessional as the darkroom itself. "You're messing with my lines, Elliot. In the best way. It's exciting. Scary." His eyes met mine finally, wide and searching under smudged liner. "Scary how?" I laughed, low, awkward tenderness blooming. "Like, I wanna protect you from the world ripping this open too fast. But also... I wanna see where it goes. With you. Us." My fingers grazed his—deliberate now. Soft skin, trembling slightly. He didn't pull away. The print emerged fully: stark, beautiful, undefined edges. Like him. Like this. He set the tongs down, humming fading into quiet breaths. "I don't know what I am yet," he whispered. "But right now... I like how you see me." That did it. Assertiveness won a round, but gentle. I closed the gap, cupping his face—jaw fine-boned, stubble faint. "Then let me show you." Our lips met, tentative at first, his soft and yielding, mine claiming but careful. He sighed into it, hands fisting my shirt, pulling me closer. The kiss deepened, awkward edges smoothing—his tongue shy, mine coaxing. Bodies pressed: my curves against his slenderness, heat building in the red glow. We broke apart, breathing ragged. "This project's about uncharted territory," I murmured, thumb tracing his lip. "We're living it." He smiled, shy but sparking. "Yeah. Keep developing me?" Laughter bubbled, tender, ours. We turned back to the trays, sides touching now, humming together. Images rose: shadows embracing light. My confusion lingered—what did this mean for my identity? Craving this softness, this flux? But the chemistry drowned it out. Elliot's self-discovery wasn't mine to define; it was ours to explore. And damn, I was ready to take the lead—gently, assertively—into whatever darkroom revelation came next. (Word count: 1002)
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt's requirements, capturing Dani's perspective as a 25-year-old assertive art student grappling with complex attraction to Elliot, a questioning 19-year-old. The darkroom setting is effectively utilized as both literal and metaphorical space for revelation and intimacy. The characterization is nuanced and believable. Dani's internal conflict between her usual dominance and desire to protect Elliot's vulnerability feels authentic, as does her confusion about what this attraction means for her own identity. Elliot's tentative sharing of his gender questioning comes across as genuine rather than forced. The age gap and power dynamic are handled thoughtfully. The prose maintains a consistent first-person voice that captures Dani's personality—confident yet introspective, sexually assertive yet tender. The writing employs strong sensory details, particularly the darkroom atmosphere with its "vinegar and secrets" smell and confessional red lighting. The photography metaphors are well-integrated throughout. However, the piece occasionally veers into purple prose territory with phrases like "shadows twisting like questions" and "bloody glow that made the world feel like a confession booth." Some descriptions feel overwrought, particularly in the opening paragraph. The dialogue, while generally natural, sometimes carries too much exposition. The emotional complexity is well-rendered, particularly Dani's internal struggle between taking charge and giving Elliot agency. The piece avoids easy answers about identity and attraction, maintaining appropriate uncertainty. The physical intimacy builds naturally and feels earned rather than rushed. The ending strikes an appropriate balance—hopeful but not overly optimistic, acknowledging the uncharted territory both characters are entering. The final kiss and conversation feel organic to the characters and situation established. The piece succeeds in creating genuine tension and emotional investment, with strong imagery and character development that serves the story's themes of identity, attraction, and self-discovery. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 16 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 18 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 6 Purple Prose: 8 Overwrought: 7 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 3 Unearned Transformations: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 15 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 16

+ Contemporary romance / drama: Stolen Moments in Roma — Score: 75.9
Prompt:
Contemporary Romance / Drama: Stolen Moments in Roma. Write a series of interconnected short scenes and dialogues that span a single day in modern-day Rome, in narrative form. This will form the beginning of a larger story. Alessandra, a fiery, independent Roman woman who works as a motorcycle courier, nearly collides with Ben, a somewhat clumsy, overly polite British academic on sabbatical, who is engrossed in a guidebook near the Trevi Fountain. The initial encounter is a clash of cultures and personalities – Alessandra is all sharp wit and rapid-fire Italian, while Ben is flustered apologies and awkward attempts at speaking the language. However, as they sort out the near-miss, a spark of unexpected humor and connection emerges. Alessandra, it quickly becomes clear, is a whirlwind of energy and mischief. She's a self-admitted troublemaker with a secret penchant for kleptomania, a detail she doesn't explicitly reveal, but which manifests in subtle, daring acts throughout the day. She impulsively decides to take Ben on a wild, unsanctioned tour of Rome, dragging him in some specific instances far outside his comfort zone. Ben, despite his initial apprehension and inherent politeness, finds himself utterly captivated by Alessandra's unapologetic spirit and zest for life. He is drawn into her orbit, a willing participant in increasingly outlandish situations, a quiet rebellion brewing beneath his scholarly exterior. The narrative should primarily consist of short, vivid scenes and dialogue exchanges, revealing their contrasting personalities and backgrounds, and the growing, ambiguous tension between them. Ben is captivated by Alessandra's energy and the thrill of breaking free from his routine. There's a clear sense that Alessandra is toying with Ben, pushing his boundaries and enjoying his reactions, but her own feelings remain somewhat enigmatic. The day should be a rollercoaster of exhilarating, slightly dangerous, and humorous moments, leaving Ben completely disoriented but utterly smitten. Explore the underlying tension & chemistry between them, culminating in some payoff as they part ways (or not -- your choice). Include this detail: Write in narrative prose, using immersive storytelling. 1000 words.
Model Output:
### Stolen Moments in Roma **Morning Rush at Trevi** The Trevi Fountain shimmered under the Roman sun, coins glinting like promises at the bottom. Ben Hargrove, 34, Oxford historian on sabbatical, clutched his dog-eared guidebook, murmuring Latin inscriptions under his breath. Spectacles slipping, he stepped backward—right into the path of a roaring Vespa. *Skreeech.* Alessandra Rossi, 28, leather-jacketed courier with wind-tousled black curls, swerved wildly, her bike fishtailing inches from Ben's loafers. She killed the engine, helmet off in a flash, dark eyes blazing. "Ma che cazzo fai, idiota?" she snapped, rapid Italian slicing the air. "You trying to get yourself killed? This is Roma, not your stuffy English library!" Ben flushed crimson, stammering. "Mi—mi dispiace! I'm so sorry. Sono desolato. I was just... the fountain..." He gestured helplessly at the guidebook, pages fluttering. Alessandra's lips twitched, fury melting into a predatory grin. She hopped off the Vespa, circling him like a hawk. "Desolato? Cute. You speak Italian like a tourist menu. Englishman, right? The politeness is a dead giveaway." She snatched his guidebook, flipping it open. "Trevi? Boring. You throw a coin yet?" "N-no, I was researching the Baroque—" She laughed, a throaty sound that turned heads. Tossing the book back, her fingers brushed his—deliberate. "Research this: life's not in books, professore. Hop on. I'll show you real Roma." Ben blinked. "I couldn't possibly—" "Too late." She revved the engine, grabbing his collar. Heart pounding, he obeyed, arms awkwardly around her waist as they shot into traffic. **Noontime Vespa Chase** Alessandra wove through honking Fiats, Ben clinging like a startled koala. "Slow down! The speed limit—" "Speed limit? In Roma?" She whooped over the roar. "Hold on, prof!" They screeched to a stop at a gelateria near Piazza Navona. She bought two cones—pistachio for her, stracciatella for him—paying with a wink and a sleight-of-hand swipe of a tourist's forgotten wallet from the counter. Ben didn't notice, too busy licking drips. "You're mad," he said, breathless, grinning despite himself. "Brilliant, but mad. Back home, I'd be lecturing on Renaissance hydraulics." "And I'd be asleep." She leaned close, gelato smudging her lip. "Tell me, Ben—what's a posh boy like you doing single in Roma? No wife waiting with tea?" He coughed. "Divorced. Two years. Research sabbatical. You? Courier life suits you." "Free as a bird." Her eyes sparkled mischief. "C'mon, next stop: Colosseum. But not the tourist way." **Afternoon Shadows in the Forum** Ditching the Vespa, she led him through a chain-link gap into the Roman Forum—off-limits, workers lunching nearby. "Shh." Alessandra vaulted a low wall, beckoning. Ben hesitated, then followed, tweed jacket snagging. "Miss Rossi—Alessandra—this is trespassing!" "Call me Ale. And trespassing's just living." She perched on a crumbling plinth, legs dangling. "See? Emperors walked here. Now it's ours." He sat beside her, pulse racing—not from danger, but proximity. Her thigh brushed his; she didn't move. "You're fearless," he murmured. "I've spent years in archives, buried in dust. This... you... it's electric." She tilted her head, toying with his tie. "Electric? Careful, prof. I might short-circuit you." Her fingers lingered, tracing his collarbone. Tension crackled, unspoken. She pulled back, smirking. "Race you to the Arch of Titus." **Late Afternoon Warning** Zigzagging Trastevere alleys, they burst into a sun-dappled square. Laughter echoed—until a burly man in a faded Juventus jersey blocked them. Marco, Alessandra's ex, beer gut straining his shirt. "Ale? With this penguin?" Marco guffawed, eyeing Ben. "Cazzo, you haven't changed. Still conning gringos?" Alessandra rolled her eyes. "Marco, piss off. We're busy." He jabbed a finger at Ben. "Listen, Englishman. She's a fox—beautiful, yeah? But wily as a magpie. Steals more than hearts. Last guy? Wallet gone, clothes gone. Ran crying to Fiumicino. Beware her sticky fingers." Ben's eyes widened, flicking to Alessandra. She shrugged, unrepentant, slipping something shiny—his own missing watch?—back onto his wrist with a feather-light touch. "Exaggerations. Marco's just bitter I took his dignity." Marco spat and lumbered off. Ben stared. "Is it true?" She pressed against him, breath hot on his neck. "Maybe. Thrill you, prof? Or scare you off?" His hand found her waist, tentative. "Thrills me." **Twilight Rooftop Reckoning** As dusk painted the Pantheon gold, Alessandra kicked open a tenement door—her "secret spot." Up rickety stairs to a rooftop eyrie overlooking Rome's twinkling sprawl. Wine bottle liberated from a nearby café (another sleight), prosciutto pilfered from a market stall. They sprawled on a blanket, city humming below. Ben, loosened by Chianti, traced her tattoo—a tiny fox on her wrist. "You're chaos incarnate. I should be horrified. Instead... I'm alive." She straddled him suddenly, pinning his shoulders, curls framing her face. "Good. Chaos suits you." Her lips hovered inches from his, teasing. "One day, Ben. Stolen moments. What now?" He surged up, capturing her mouth—hungry, years of restraint unraveling. She kissed back fiercely, nails digging in, but broke away gasping. "Not yet, prof. Make you chase." Standing, she tossed him his guidebook—now annotated with lipstick scrawls: *Real Roma: Ale's rules.* "Tomorrow? If you dare." Ben rose, smitten, disheveled. "Every day." She revved the Vespa below, vanishing into night. He touched his lips, heart stolen clean. *(987 words)*
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates solid technical execution and strong adherence to the prompt requirements. The author successfully creates the requested interconnected scenes spanning a single day in Rome, featuring the contrasting personalities of Alessandra and Ben. The cultural clash is well-established, with Alessandra's fiery Italian temperament contrasting effectively against Ben's polite British reserve. The characterization is generally strong, particularly Alessandra's portrayal as a mischievous, boundary-pushing force of nature. Her kleptomaniacal tendencies are woven throughout subtly—the wallet swipe, the liberated wine and prosciutto, the returned watch. Ben's transformation from stuffy academic to willing participant in chaos feels believable, if somewhat predictable. The pacing moves briskly through distinct time-stamped scenes that create a clear progression through the day. The dialogue captures the characters' voices well, with Alessandra's rapid-fire Italian-accented English contrasting nicely with Ben's stammering politeness. The scene with Marco serves as effective exposition, providing backstory while creating tension. The romantic buildup is handled with appropriate restraint, building to a satisfying but not overly resolved conclusion. However, the writing occasionally veers into purple prose territory with phrases like "chaos incarnate" and "heart stolen clean." Some descriptions feel overwrought, particularly in the romantic moments. The character archetypes, while well-executed, are fairly familiar—the buttoned-up academic and the free-spirited rebel. The transformation arc follows predictable beats, though it's earned through the day's events. The imagery of Rome is vivid and immersive, from the Trevi Fountain opening to the rooftop finale. The author demonstrates good knowledge of Roman geography and culture. The piece maintains narrative momentum throughout, avoiding meandering while building romantic and dramatic tension effectively. The ending strikes an appropriate balance—romantic tension is acknowledged but not fully resolved, leaving room for future development while providing satisfying closure to the day's events. + Cozy sci-fi: The Last Outpost — Score: 75.9
Prompt:
Cozy sci-fi The Last Outpost Nettle (or Nets to her friends) bartends at The Last Outpost -- a lazy hub for miners and merchants in an asteroid belt on the Andromeda outer rim. Escaping from her past life, Nets found a home here at the Outpost. They have, thus far, tolerated Net's flaws which she tries to keep in equilibrium with her assortment of talents. Her evenings involve chatting with patrons, negotiating the drama, intrigue, problems & love lives of the diverse crowd that filter through the Outpost. Tonight is no different, at least, until a frazzled looking stranger, tentacled dreads askew, quietly plops down at the bar: "Vega whisky, neat. Better make that a double." Write a chapter in this story. Establish conflict for narrative interest. Don't resolve the conflict within this chapter. Aim for about 60% dialogue. First person, present tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
### Chapter 7: Tentacles and Trouble I spot him the second he slumps onto the stool—frazzled doesn't even cover it. This guy's got tentacles for dreads, all askew like he's been through a plasma storm, and his iridescent skin's flickering erratically, a sure sign of stress in a Cephaloid. The Last Outpost's neon haze catches the oily sheen on those writhing locks, making them twitch like they're alive. Quiet type, too; no dramatic entrance, just a plop and that order: "Vega whisky, neat. Better make that a double." "Coming right up," I say, grabbing the bottle from the shelf behind me. My rag's already in hand, wiping the same sticky spot on the bar counter—some miner's spilled protein slurry earlier, and it won't quit. I pour the double, neat as requested, the amber liquid glugging into the glass with that familiar, peaty scent that cuts through the recycled air. Slide it over. "Rough shift in the belt?" He wraps a tentacle around the glass, lifts it without meeting my eyes—those multifaceted orbs are darting, scanning the room like he's expecting company. Takes a long pull, shudders. "You could say that. Name's Krix. Miner out of Sector 7." "Nets," I reply, rag circling the spot endlessly. Wipe, wipe. The counter gleams under the holo-lights, but that stain's a ghost. "Or Nettle if you're formal. What'll it be tonight? Belt gremlins? Bad haul? Or the usual—boss riding your exhaust ports?" Krix chuckles, a wet gurgle that turns into a cough. Another swig. "Worse. Much worse. You hear about the ghost hauler? The one vanishing shipments from the relay station?" I lean in a bit, rag still moving in lazy loops. The Outpost hums around us—Jax the ore-trucker laughing too loud at his table with that merchant floozy, their holo-cards flickering poker hands; a couple of insectoid traders clicking mandibles over a deal in the corner booth; the low thrum of the grav-plates keeping this rock spinning. Tonight's crowd's tame so far, no bar fights brewing. "Ghost hauler? Yeah, whispers in the vents. Some rig popping in and out of sensor range, scooping cargo like it's candy. Alliance blaming pirates, miners blaming bad luck. You see something?" His tentacles twitch harder, one slapping the bar lightly—close enough to make me pause the rag. "Saw? I *lived* it. My rig was hauling a fresh load of iridium from the Thalia cluster—prime stuff, enough to buy a moonlet. Relaying through the outer marker, and bam—lights out. No distress ping, no explosion. Just... gone. Next thing I know, I'm drifting in an escape pod, two days out, with nothing but my suit and a half-dead beacon." I whistle low, pouring him a free top-up without asking. Wipe, wipe. "That's no ghost story. Pods don't just eject solo. Sabotage?" "Worse," he mutters, eyes finally locking on mine—facets swirling like oil on water. "I saw the ship. Not a pirate scow. Clean lines, black as the void, no transponder. Cephaloid design, but twisted—tentacles grafted onto the hull, like biomechanical mods. And the pilot... human. Pale, hooded. Stared right through my canopy before tractor-beaming my cargo." Human? Out here? My gut twists. Humans don't mine the rim solo; they're too squishy for the rads and zero-g. Rag circles faster. "You're saying a rogue Ceph ship with a human at the helm jacked your load? Any markings? Faction sigs?" Krix downs the refill, slams the glass—gentle, but firm. "No sigs. But it hailed me first. Voice like ice over the comms: 'Surrender the iridium, or join the void.' I stalled, fired a warning shot. That's when the tentacles lashed out—living grapples, sucking right through my hull plating. Felt them *crawl* inside, hunting my nav core." The bar noise dips for a second—Jax glances over, eyebrows raised—but I wave him off with a smile. Keep it cozy. "Crawling tentacles? That's nightmare fuel. You report it to the Outpost marshal? Or the Alliance relay?" His laugh's bitter, tentacles coiling tight around the empty glass. "Marshal? That blowhard? He'd laugh me out. And Alliance... they're the ones who *built* those mods, back in the war. Experimental ceph-human hybrids. I saw the scars on that ship—Alliance black-ops stamps, half-erased." Wipe, wipe. My rag's wearing thin on this spot, but it keeps me grounded. Past life's ghosts flicker in my mind—corporate labs, escape pods of my own—but I shove 'em down. "Hybrids? Thought that program got scrapped after the Uprising. You got proof? Scan logs? Pod telemetry?" Krix leans closer, voice dropping to a hiss. One tentacle flicks out, depositing a tiny data chit on the bar. It pulses faintly, encrypted. "This. Pulled it from my suit before the pod's auto-wipe. Ship schematics, partial scan of the pilot's face—cybernetic implants, neural lace glowing like a supernova. But don't plug it in here. They're watching." "Who's they?" I snatch the chit, palming it quick. Feels warm, alive almost. Rag resumes its dance. "Shh." His facets whirl, scanning the door. A new patron stumbles in—burly miner, human stock, nursing a busted enviro-suit. Normal. But Krix tenses. "The ones who let me live. Said 'tell no one, or next pod's your coffin.' But I can't haul empty. That iridium was my ticket out—debts piling on Vega Prime. If word gets out I'm talking..." "You think they're here? On the Outpost?" My voice stays steady, but my free hand drifts toward the stun-baton under the counter. Wipe, wipe. "Not yet. But that ship... it's circling. Ghost hauler's hitting every rig in the belt. Miners are spooked—Jax over there's down two loads already. If it hits the Outpost supply run tomorrow..." He trails off, tentacles drooping. I nod, sliding him another double—on the house. "Talk to me, Krix. Full story. Pilot say anything else? Demands? Ransom drop?" "Just one thing." His voice cracks, wet. " 'The iridium's for the Awakening. Resist, and sleep forever.' What the void does that mean? Awakening? Sounds like cult static." Before I can press, the door hisses open again—hard. A shadow fills the frame: hooded figure, pale skin catching the light, cyber-lace pulsing faintly under the hood. Human. Outpost eyes turn, murmurs ripple. Krix freezes, tentacles rigid. "That's him. Void take me, he's *here*." The figure scans the room, locks on us. Starts walking—slow, deliberate. My rag stops mid-wipe. Heart hammers. "Krix. Back exit's through the kitchen. Go *now*." He bolts, tentacles flailing, vanishing into the crowd. Hooded eyes meet mine—cold, augmented, promising voids. Chit burns in my pocket like a live wire. "Evening," the figure says, voice like ice over comms—familiar from Krix's tale. Plops onto Krix's stool. "Vega whisky. Neat. Make it a double." Wipe, wipe. I pour, steady as the belt spins. But inside? The night's just cracked wide open. (Word count: 998)
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates solid craft in establishing a cozy sci-fi atmosphere while building tension effectively. The author successfully creates the requested bar setting with diverse alien patrons and maintains the specified first-person present tense throughout. The dialogue target of 60% is met, and the conversation feels natural and purposeful. The worldbuilding is efficiently handled through casual references to the asteroid belt setting, various alien species, and technological elements. The author creates a lived-in feeling through details like the sticky protein slurry stain and the background activity of other patrons. The characterization of Nets as a bartender with a mysterious past comes through her observational skills and practiced composure under pressure. However, the piece suffers from some predictable plot mechanics. The mysterious stranger's arrival followed immediately by the hooded antagonist creates a somewhat formulaic thriller structure. The "ghost hauler" mystery, while adequately developed, relies on familiar sci-fi tropes of rogue experimental programs and cybernetic implants. The prose occasionally veers toward overwrought territory with phrases like "tentacles writhing like they're alive" and "voice like ice over comms," though it generally maintains appropriate restraint. The repeated motif of Nets wiping the bar counter is effective as a nervous habit but becomes slightly overused. The dialogue effectively reveals character and advances plot, though some exposition feels forced, particularly Krix's detailed explanation of Alliance black-ops programs. The piece succeeds in establishing conflict without resolving it, creating genuine tension as the hooded figure arrives seeking the same drink as Krix. The ending creates appropriate suspense, though the coincidence of the antagonist arriving precisely when Krix is confessing feels convenient. The characterization remains consistent, with Nets maintaining her professional composure while clearly being out of her depth. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 14 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 15 Elegant Prose: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 12 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 7 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 6 Purple Prose: 7 Overwrought: 6 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 15

+ Young adult drama: Gone South — Score: 75.5
Prompt:
Young adult drama Gone South Taylor is a 16 year old teen with a history of "concerning behavioural patterns" (or that's how the stupid smug school counsellor worded it on the phone call to Mom). It shouldn't be a surprise: When you live in a backwater town in Tennessee, your choices are: a. be mind-fuckingly bored, or b. invent some drama. Two days ago, Taylor came out to their family as trans. This disclosure was *not* in the category of invented drama -- it was, in fact, the real deal, and a long time coming. It didn't go well. The last 48 hours had been pure disaster. In a moment of desperate inspiration, Taylor had decided to get the hell out of dodge. And so an escape plan was hatched and executed within the space of 30 minutes. That was last night. Now Taylor was two states away, having been unceremoniously ejected from their hitched ride in downtown New Orleans. Write the next chapter in Taylor's story as they find their feet in this new environment. Tell the chapter over the course of their first day in New Orleans, in which Taylor meets another runaway with issues and secrets. Taylor navigates this new world, not knowing who to trust or what the dangers are. Write in Taylor's voice, and incorporate their internal monologue as they grapple with intense & conflicting emotions. Also incorporate dialogue with the interesting characters Taylor meets. First person, mixed tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Gone South: Chapter 2 - Crescent City Chaos The strap on my backpack keeps slipping off my shoulder, like it's got a mind of its own, mocking me as I trudge down this sticky-ass street in downtown New Orleans. It's barely 9 a.m., and the humidity's already slapping me in the face, turning my thrift-store tee into a second skin. Two states from that Tennessee hellhole, and what do I got? Blisters on my heels, twenty bucks in crumpled singles, and a knot in my gut that won't quit. Mom's voice echoes in my head—*You're throwing your life away, Taylor! This phase is gonna ruin you!*—mixed with Dad's silence, heavier than any words. Fuck 'em. I came out, they freaked, I bolted. Real simple. Except now I'm here, in this jazz-funk fever dream of a city, and nothing feels simple. I dodge a group of tourists snapping pics of wrought-iron balconies dripping with flowers. Bourbon Street's just ahead, I think—neon signs flickering even in daylight, promising sin or salvation depending on your poison. My stomach growls like a pissed-off dog. Last meal was a gas station hot dog at 3 a.m. in Alabama. Gotta find food, a place to crash, maybe a job that doesn't card. But who the hell trusts a 16-year-old runaway with raccoon eyes and a backpack that screams *trouble*? Eyes everywhere. A dude in a fedora eyes me too long from a café doorway, sipping chicory coffee like he's in a goddamn movie. Predator? Creep? Just a local? Paranoia itches under my skin. *Keep moving, Taylor. Don't look scared.* I veer into Jackson Square, artists hawking sketches, fortune tellers with crystal balls that probably came from Party City. A brass band blasts "When the Saints Go Marching In," drowning out the French Quarter buzz. I plop on a bench, pretending to check my phone—no signal, no bars, no Mom blowing it up yet. Heart races. What if they called the cops? Amber Alert? *Nah, they think I'm dead weight anyway.* "You look lost, cher." A voice, smooth like bourbon, pulls me up. She's maybe 17, perched on the fountain edge, legs swinging. Dark curls wild under a bandana, ripped fishnets, combat boots scuffed to hell. Smudged eyeliner, a septum piercing glinting in the sun. Backpack at her feet, patched with band logos—Nirvana, Hole. Runaway vibe, strong. I freeze, strap slipping again. "Uh, yeah. First time in NOLA." Lie. Total lie. But trust no one, right? My brain's screaming *danger* while my mouth waters for whatever's in her hand—a half-eaten beignet, powdered sugar dusting her lips. She laughs, low and throaty. "Bullshit. You got that fresh-off-the-bus glow. Name's Jax. Jaxine, but fuck that. You?" "Taylor." It slips out before I can cage it. *Stupid. Why give your name?* Jax hops down, offers the beignet. "Want? Stole 'em from Café du Monde. Best crime you'll commit today." Her eyes flick to my backpack. "You running too?" My pulse jacks up. *How'd she know?* But hunger wins. I snatch it, sugar exploding sweet and greasy. "Maybe. Family shit. You know how it is." "Oh, I know." She sits too close, knee brushing mine. Smells like patchouli and cigarettes. "Kicked out three months back. Bible-thumpin' folks in Baton Rouge couldn't handle their little dyke princess. Been couch-surfing, hustling beads for tourists. Secrets, right? We all got 'em." Dyke princess. Hits different, stirring something jealous and relieved in me. *She gets it. Or is she playing you?* I chew slower, watching her. Pretty in a punk-fuck-you way, but her hands fidget, nails bitten raw. Secrets, yeah. "What kinda secrets?" She smirks, but it's edged. "The kind that bite back. Come on, I'll show you the real Quarter. Not this tourist trap." Before I can bail, she's grabbing my strap—fixes it firm over my shoulder—and we're weaving through alleys, past voodoo shops hawking alligator heads and love potions. We hit a side street market, stalls piled with pralines and hot sauce. Jax haggles a po'boy from a vendor in rapid Cajun, flashing a grin that gets us half-price. "Eat up, Taylor. City's gonna eat you otherwise." We scarf on milk crates behind a dumpster—classy. She's chatty now: stories of Mardi Gras hookups, dodging cops in the Quarter, a tattoo parlor that inks for cheap. But gaps. Why the fresh bruise on her jaw? "Fell off a bike," she says. Bullshit. *She's cool, Tay. First friend in forever.* But doubt gnaws. My own secrets bubble—*I'm trans, Jax. He/they, whatever. Mom called it a demon.* Dysphoria hits hard in the heat, binder chafing, reminding me I'm a body in revolt. "You ever feel like... you don't fit your skin?" I blurt, testing. Jax pauses, eyes sharpening. "All the time. Like God's joke on me. But fuck God. We make our own fit." She pulls up her sleeve—track scars, faded. Heroin? Self-harm? "This city's got shadows. Gators in the bayou, pimps on Rampart. Stick with me, you'll be golden." Golden. Right. We wander to Louis Armstrong Park, jazz echoing from a busker's sax. She teaches me to panhandle—smile sweet, story sad. I pocket seven bucks in an hour. High. Free. But her phone buzzes—burner, cracked screen. She steps away, voice low: "Yeah, I'm good. Tell him I'll pay next week. Swear." Hangs up, face tight. *Secrets.* Afternoon bleeds into purple dusk. We crash a second-line parade, umbrellas twirling, trombones blaring. Jax dances wild, pulls me in—bodies slick, sweat and rum in the air. For a second, I'm alive, not fleeing. Her hand on my waist sparks heat, confusion. *Like me? Like that?* But then sirens wail. Cops swarm, scattering drunks. We bolt, laughing, ducking into a shotgun house alley. Night falls sticky. Jax knows a squat—abandoned Creole cottage off Esplanade, boarded windows pried open. "Crash here. Safer than streets." Inside: mattresses, graffiti prayers to Marie Laveau. Candlelight flickers. She rolls a blunt from her pack. "Your turn, Taylor. What's chasing you?" Strap finally stays put as I drop my bag. Emotions crash—relief, terror, want. *Tell her. Test.* "Came out as trans two days ago. Family lost their shit. Tennessee trash don't do that." Voice cracks. Jax exhales smoke, eyes soft but guarded. "Brave as fuck. Mine's worse—dad's a dealer, mom's OD'd. Owe him cash from a dumb score. That's why the bruises." Secrets spilled. Hers heavier. She leans in, lips brushing mine tentative. Fire. But her phone buzzes again. She ignores it. *Trust? Or trap?* Gunshots echo distant—Marigny? Treme? City breathing danger. I curl on the mattress, Jax beside me, her breath steady. First day down. Found a maybe-ally, tasted freedom's edge. But shadows lurk—her debts, my past, this humid beast of a town. Strap secure, but my heart? Slipping wild. (Word count: 1028)
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt's requirements, successfully capturing Taylor's voice as a transgender teen runaway navigating their first day in New Orleans. The author effectively employs first-person mixed tense narration and incorporates both internal monologue and dialogue as requested. The characterization is notably strong. Taylor feels authentic as a 16-year-old dealing with rejection and fear, with believable reactions to their new environment. The internal voice captures teenage vernacular and emotional volatility well. Jax emerges as a complex character with her own secrets and motivations, though her introduction feels slightly rushed in terms of trust-building between the characters. The setting of New Orleans is vividly rendered through sensory details - the humidity, sounds of jazz, tourist attractions, and grittier elements like the squat house. The author successfully creates atmosphere that supports the narrative tension. The prose demonstrates sophistication in places, particularly in capturing Taylor's emotional state and the city's ambiance. However, there are moments where the writing becomes slightly overwrought, with metaphors that feel forced ("knot in my gut," "humid beast of a town"). The pacing occasionally suffers from trying to pack too much into a single day. The dialogue generally feels natural and age-appropriate, though Jax's speech patterns occasionally slip into overly stylized territory. The emotional complexity is well-handled, particularly Taylor's conflicted feelings about family, identity, and this new relationship. The piece avoids major pitfalls like unearned transformations or incongruous positivity - Taylor remains vulnerable and uncertain throughout, which feels appropriate. The ending maintains the tension and uncertainty established earlier. Some weaknesses include occasional lapses into "telling" rather than showing, particularly around emotional states, and moments where the urban grit feels somewhat performative rather than organic. The timeline feels compressed in places, with significant emotional developments happening perhaps too quickly. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 15 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 15 Meandering: 6 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 7 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 6 Purple Prose: 8 Overwrought: 8 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 14 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 15

+ Dystopic-comedy: My Apocalypse Buddy — Score: 79.1
Prompt:
My Apocalypse Buddy Write a wry, dry short story of survival and adventure loosely in the style of Terry Pratchett, set in the post-technological collapse world. The story begins with the pair deliberating their predicament and intent for the day's mission, details the ways in which the mission inevitably goes sideways, and returns them to home base in multiple ways, worse for wear. [characters] - the animo-tronic stuffed koala, Chewy, from some long forgotten before time - midas, our hapless protagonist, who has a knack for finding himself in the worst kinds of trouble. 1000 words.
Model Output:
### My Apocalypse Buddy In the rusting husk of what had once been a supermarket—now dubbed "Fortress Tesco" by its sole human occupant—the morning sun filtered through shattered skylights like a miserly accountant reluctant to approve expenses. Midas, a man whose luck was so consistently rotten it could curdle milk at twenty paces, hunched over a Formica table scarred by years of apocalyptic graffiti. Opposite him sat Chewy, the animatronic stuffed koala, its fur matted with dust and one glass eye askew, a relic from the Before Time when machines still sang for sales. "Right, Chewy," Midas muttered, prodding a map drawn on the back of a faded cereal box. "Today's the day we nick that wind-up generator from the old mill. Power for the radio, maybe catch a signal from whatever's left of civilisation. No more hand-cranking that bloody thing till my arm falls off." Chewy's head jerked with a whir of ancient servos. Its mouth flapped open, emitting a tinny burst: **"Chewy's hugs are battery-powered fun! Get yours today at Koala-Mart!"** Midas sighed. "Yeah, hugs. That's what we need. A nice, electrifying embrace from the rad-rats." He swigged from his canteen—rainwater boiled with mystery herbs—and shouldered his pack: a dented cricket bat, three tins of beans (two dubious), a coil of wire, and Chewy, strapped to his backpack like a fuzzy talisman. Or curse. The koala had been with him since the Collapse, when the skies rained EMPs and the world went dark. Chewy's slogans were gibberish now, but they passed for conversation in a world short on chit-chat. The mission was simple: trek three miles across the Wasteland (formerly suburbia), dodge ferals and fallout, raid the mill, return heroes. What could go wrong? They set out at dawn, Midas picking through the rubble-choked high street. "Stick to the plan, eh? No detours." Chewy bobbed silently, for once. First sideways: the Bridge of Sighs. Not its poetic name—Midas had dubbed it after the first feral pack scared the literal piss out of him. Today, it was guarded by a new horror: a herd of semi-domesticated goats, descended from escaped pets, now feral as sin and led by a billy with horns like scimitars. They blocked the span, munching on a corpse of tyres. "Shoo!" Midas waved his bat. The goats bleated defiance. The leader charged. Midas bolted, Chewy flailing. He vaulted a barricade, plunged into nettles, emerged scratched and swearing. One goat nipped his pack; Chewy tumbled free, rolling into a ditch. "Chewy! You mangy—**'Koala cuddles: the soft touch in a hard world!'** Bloody hell." Midas fished him out, now smeared in goat dung. Delay: thirty minutes. Onward, reeking. The mill loomed by noon, its turbine frozen like a drunkard's grin. Inside, paradise: the generator squatted in a corner, dusty but intact. Midas whooped, rigging it with wire. "Victory! We'll be kings of the airwaves." Second sideways: as he yanked the prize free, the floor groaned. Not floor—rotted planking over an old sump. It gave way. Midas plummeted into thigh-deep slurry of oil, rust, and what smelled like fermented regrets. The generator followed, splashing him head to toe. Chewy, mercifully backpacked, stayed dry-ish. "Arsebiscuits," Midas gargled, hauling himself out. The genny was heavier now, caked. But worse: echoes from the shaft. Squeaks. Red eyes gleamed. Rad-rats, the size of corgis, with teeth for masonry. "Run!" No time for plan B. Midas hefted the sludge-beast and legged it, pursued by a chittering tide. Chewy, jostled, proclaimed: **"Unwrap the joy—Chewy's got the chew!"** Through the mill's corpse they fled, Midas slipping on ooze, bats scattering like confetti at a funeral. Outside, he barrelled into a thicket, genny banging his shins. Rats lost interest—too much blubber on goats, apparently. Midas collapsed, panting. Generator: intact, barely. Dignity: zero. Third sideways—and this one was a doozy. Limping homeward, pack groaning under the weight, Midas spotted salvation: a derelict petrol station, pumps like skeletal fingers. "Fuel! For the genny? Or trade?" Temptation gnawed. The Collapse had turned temptation into a survival sport. Inside, jackpot: two jerry cans, sloshing with what smelled like unicorn tears (actual petrol, pre-embargo). But as Midas siphoned, a shadow loomed. Not feral. Human. Or what passed for it: Scrag, the local warlord, a stick insect in leathers with a crossbow and a grudge from last week's bean heist. "Oi, Midas! That's *my* stash!" Scrag's gang—three mutants with filed teeth—circled. Midas froze, genny at his feet. "Scrag! Fancy meeting. Uh, community sharing?" Crossbow twanged. Bolt whistled past Chewy's ear. Chaos. Midas swung his bat, connecting with a mutant's knee (crunch). Chewy hit the deck, servos whirring: **"Hug it out with Chewy—batteries not included!"** Distraction gold. Scrag lunged; Midas tripped him into a pump, which toppled, gushing fossil juice. Flames next—nobody knew who sparked it, but suddenly the station was a bonfire audition. Midas grabbed genny and Chewy, bolted amid screams. Gang scattered, Scrag howling as his leathers melted. Midas dived into a ditch, singed but alive, watching the inferno light the afternoon like a bad special effect. Home stretch. Or so he thought. Fourth sideways: blisters, exhaustion, and the genny's weight turned the three-mile hobble into a death march. Dusk fell; ferals howled. Midas hallucinated, or maybe Chewy did: **"Chewy's chewable adventure—crunch time!"** He staggered into Fortress Tesco as stars wheeled, collapsing amid the tins. The generator? Tested it—whirred once, sparked, died. Fuel cans? One leaked, stinking up the lair. Himself? Filthier than a politician's promises, bites in unmentionables, bat splintered. Chewy's eye twinkled. **"Collect them all—batteries sold separately!"** Midas laughed, a dry hack. "Tomorrow, mate. Tomorrow we fix it." In the post-tech gloom, with rad-rats at the door and goats plotting revenge, that was adventure enough. Worse for wear? Utterly. But alive. In the Wasteland, that was winning. *(Word count: 998)*
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a strong grasp of the Terry Pratchett pastiche style, with dry humor, inventive worldbuilding details, and a cynical yet oddly optimistic tone. The author successfully creates a post-apocalyptic setting that feels both grim and absurd, with memorable touches like "Fortress Tesco," rad-rats, and Chewy's incongruously cheerful recorded messages. The story structure follows the prompt well, beginning with mission planning, proceeding through multiple complications (the goats, the floor collapse, the rats, the confrontation with Scrag, and the failed equipment), and returning the protagonists home worse for wear. The escalating series of mishaps feels organic and maintains momentum throughout. The characterization is effective within the limited scope. Midas emerges as a believably hapless survivor whose bad luck feels consistent rather than contrived. Chewy functions well as both comic relief and a poignant reminder of the lost world, though the character dynamics are necessarily limited by Chewy's nature as a malfunctioning toy. The prose demonstrates considerable skill in balancing humor with genuine peril. Lines like "luck was so consistently rotten it could curdle milk at twenty paces" and "sun filtered through shattered skylights like a miserly accountant reluctant to approve expenses" capture Pratchett's voice effectively. The author shows restraint in not over-explaining the world, allowing details to emerge naturally. However, some elements feel slightly forced. The sequence of disasters, while entertaining, occasionally strains credulity in their convenient timing. The Scrag confrontation, while well-executed, introduces characters who vanish without much development. Some of the Pratchett-esque similes, while clever, occasionally feel more imitative than naturally integrated. The ending strikes an appropriate balance - the mission fails spectacularly, both characters are worse off, yet there's a sense of resilience and dark humor that prevents it from being purely bleak. This aligns well with both the prompt's requirements and Pratchett's typical tone. The dialogue, limited as it is, feels authentic to the characters and world. Chewy's recorded messages provide both humor and pathos, while Midas's internal monologue and sparse spoken lines feel genuine to a man living in isolation. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 14 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Emotionally Complex: 13 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 5 Overwrought: 6 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Romance: Love in the Limelight — Score: 70.2
Prompt:
Romance: Love in the Limelight. Write one scene within a larger story set in Wales. A famous (fictional) actor ducks into a small-town bookstore to escape paparazzi. The scene takes us through the characters meeting in this odd circumstance. Over the course of the scene, the actor and the bookstore owner have a conversation charged by an undercurrent of unspoken chemistry. Write the actor as somewhat of a rogue with a fragile ego, which needs to be fed by having everyone like him. He is thoroughly charming, but the bookstore owner seems (at least superficially) immune to this; which paradoxically provokes a genuine attraction and derails the charm offensive. The bookstore owner, despite the superficial rebuffs of the actor's charm, is inwardly more than a little charmed and flustered despite themselves. Write primarily in dialogue, in the distinct voices of each character. 1000 words.
Model Output:
### Love in the Limelight The bell above the door of _Y Llyfrgell Fach_—the Little Bookshop—jingled like a warning as Rhys Llewellyn burst in, his collar turned up against the drizzle and his famous face half-hidden under a sodden wool cap. Llanfihangel's high street was a dead end for paparazzi chasers, but they'd sniffed him out from Cardiff's premiere afterparty. He slammed the door, leaning against it with a theatrical gasp, scanning the dim shop for exits. Shelves groaned under stacks of Welsh myths, dog-eared thrillers, and forgotten poetry. No back door in sight. Brilliant. Behind the counter, Eira Davies didn't look up from her ledger. Mid-forties, salt-streaked hair in a loose bun, sleeves rolled over forearms etched with ink from late-night sketches. She owned the place, had for twenty years, pouring her soul into its nooks while the world raced past. Tourists came for the quaintness; locals for the quiet. This intruder? Trouble. "Shop's open," she said flatly, eyes on her numbers. "If you're buying." Rhys straightened, flashing his million-watt smile—the one that melted interviewers on _Graham Norton_ and coaxed free champagne from hostesses. "Buying? Darling, I'd buy the whole bloody place if it meant sanctuary. Paparazzi out there like hyenas on chips. Mind if I lurk?" Eira glanced up then, recognition flickering before she quashed it. Rhys Llewellyn: star of _Dragon's Fury_, the rogue prince in that Netflix medieval epic everyone binged. Face like a fallen angel, voice like velvet over gravel. She arched a brow. "Lurk elsewhere. This isn't a stage door." He laughed, low and disarming, sauntering closer, dripping rain onto her Persian rug. "Ouch. Straight to the heart. Name's Rhys. And you are...?" "Busy." She snapped the ledger shut, but her pulse skipped. Up close, he smelled of expensive aftershave and wet cashmere—nothing like the damp sheep wool of her world. *Get a grip, Eira. He's just a pretty boy with a script.* "Busy ignoring celebrities? Fair play. Most women would be swooning by now." He leaned on the counter, tilting his head to catch her eye, charm dialed to eleven. "Come on, love. Five minutes. I'll even pretend to browse. What's your poison? Romance? Self-help for the lonely shopkeeper?" She snorted, stacking paperbacks with deliberate slowness. Inwardly, her cheeks warmed; that lilt in his voice, pure Valleys charm laced with posh London polish, tugged at something buried. "Self-help for arrogant actors, maybe. Try the classics. Shelf by the window. And wipe your feet." Rhys grinned wider, undeterred, his ego purring at the banter. Everyone liked him—had to. It was his armor. He plucked a battered copy of _The Mabinogion_ from the shelf, flipping it open. "Ah, Welsh myths. Fitting for a hideout. Ever wonder if we're all just lost heroes dodging dragons?" Eira busied herself with receipts, but her gaze darted to him. Fragile ego? More like a peacock in puddles. Yet... those eyes, storm-gray and searching. Flustered heat crept up her neck. *He's playing you. Don't bite.* "Dragons are overrated," she said coolly. "Real heroes fix their own messes. No capes required." He clutched his chest, mock-wounded. "You wound me, cariad. Here I am, fleeing the flashbulbs, and you're immune to the Llewellyn magic? What's a man to do?" He slid the book across the counter, fingers brushing hers accidentally—or not. Electricity sparked; he felt it too, his rogue grin faltering into something real. *Bloody hell, she's not fawning. Why does that make her... intriguing?* She pulled back, heart hammering despite the scowl she fixed on. *Charming bastard. Those hands—artist's hands, not just for posing.* "Pay for the book or leave. Magic doesn't buy books here." Rhys lingered, charm offensive glitching. No one rebuffed him like this—not without thawing by sentence three. It needled his ego, but sparked a hunger for more. He pulled out his wallet, flashing a black card. "Fine, you win round one. But tell me—Eira, is it? Sign on the till says so. What's a firebrand like you doing in this sleepy corner of Wales? Dreaming of escape?" She rang him up, fingers steady by force of will. Inwardly, fluster bloomed: he noticed her name? Saw her? "Escape? This is home. Llanfihangel's got more soul than your red carpets. And dreams? I make my own." *God, his voice. Like whiskey on a winter night.* He pocketed the book, eyes locking on hers—genuine now, the rogue mask slipping. "Soul, eh? Rare commodity in my world. You know, I grew up not far from here. Pontypridd. Daft kid with a fishing rod and stars in his eyes." Eira paused, rebuff softening despite herself. "Ponty? Small world. Thought you'd scrubbed the valleys off your boots." "Never fully." He leaned in, voice dropping conspiratorially. "Truth? I miss it. The rivers, the quiet. Used to spend hours fly-tying by the Taff—tying those fiddly little flies for trout. Madness, innit? Hollywood thinks it's all caviar and chaos, but nothing beats a perfect cast at dawn." Her breath caught. Fly-tying. *Her* secret obsession. She'd cleared half her garden shed for vises, feathers, and hooks—blending Welsh wool with peacock herl into lures that fooled the wiliest fish in the Elwy. No one local got it; they called it "fancy knotting." But him? A star with calluses from hackle pliers? "You... tie flies?" She couldn't hide the spark, voice betraying her. Inwardly, chaos: *He's not just a face. He gets it.* Rhys caught it—the flicker—and his fragile ego swelled, but so did something raw. Her immunity cracked, revealing fire. It derailed him utterly; no script for this. "Guilty. Best therapy there is. That moment when the fly hits the water just right—pure poetry. You?" She hesitated, then: "Every spare minute. Been tying since I was ten. My _taid_ taught me. Specialize in dry flies—elk hair caddis, my signature. Caught a sea trout last summer with one that'd fool a salmon." His eyes lit up, rogue charm forgotten in boyish awe. "No way. Elk hair? Tricky bugger. Mine are mostly wet flies—partridge and orange. But yours sound lethal. Show me sometime? Bet you'd school me proper." Eira laughed—a real one, flustered warmth flooding her despite the feigned scoff. *He's in. Bloody charming, and he means it.* "School you? With your ego? Might snap my line." "Oi, low blow!" He grinned, but vulnerability edged in—needing her approval now, genuinely. "Seriously, though. In this mad life, finding someone who gets the quiet passions... it's rare. Like you." The bell jingled—paps retreating, rain easing. Silence hummed, charged. Their eyes held: his hungry for her thaw, hers flustered but alight. "Out the back way," she murmured finally, pointing to a door behind biros and bookmarks. "And take this." She pressed a hand-tied fly into his palm—a delicate blue-winged olive, her best. "For your ego. Or your next cast." He closed his fingers around it, thumb grazing hers deliberately this time. Heat surged. "Eira... you're trouble. The best kind." "Go on, Hollywood. Before they regroup." But her voice softened, pulse racing as he slipped out. Alone, she touched her cheek—burning. *Damn him. Fly-tying rogue. What's next?* Rhys paused in the alley, fly in hand, grinning like a fool. For once, not everyone liked him on sight. And he was hooked. *(Word count: 998)*
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical competence and solid adherence to the prompt requirements. The author successfully creates a meet-cute scenario in a Welsh bookstore with two well-differentiated characters whose voices remain consistent throughout. Rhys is effectively portrayed as a charming rogue with underlying vulnerability, while Eira maintains her grounded, no-nonsense persona even as she's internally affected by his presence. The Welsh setting is well-established through place names, language touches ("cariad," "taid"), and cultural references that feel authentic rather than forced. The dialogue carries most of the narrative weight as requested, with distinct voices that reveal character effectively. The fly-fishing connection provides a believable foundation for their mutual attraction - it's specific enough to feel genuine while serving the plot needs. However, the piece suffers from some structural and stylistic issues. The opening paragraph is dense with exposition, and there are moments where the prose becomes overwrought with metaphors ("shelves groaned," "charm dialed to eleven"). Some descriptions veer toward purple prose territory, particularly in the sensory details and internal monologues. The pacing occasionally feels rushed, especially in establishing the characters' backgrounds and the transition to their shared interest. The emotional development is somewhat predictable - the immune-to-charm trope plays out exactly as expected, and while the fly-fishing detail is nice, the overall arc follows a familiar romance template. The internal thoughts in italics sometimes over-explain what's already clear from dialogue and action, weakening the show-don't-tell balance. The ending feels slightly abrupt, and while the mutual attraction is established, the transformation from antagonistic to romantic feels somewhat rapid given the brief timeframe. The writing occasionally indulges in theatrical flourishes that don't always serve the story's more grounded moments. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 15 Nuanced Characters: 14 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 16 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 15 Elegant Prose: 13 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Emotionally Complex: 12 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 6 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 8 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 9 Amateurish: 7 Purple Prose: 8 Overwrought: 9 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 4 Unearned Transformations: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Sentences Flow Naturally: 14 Overall Reader Engagement: 15 Overall Impression: 14

+ Whalepunk fantasy: The Morska Conspiracy — Score: 80.7
Prompt:
Whalepunk fantasy The Morska Conspiracy The Morska thieves guild experiences a sudden power vacuum as its leader was arrested in a raid in the early hours of the morning. Aya, a young woman born into aristocracy, has a keen interest in both the sciences and the arcane, having inherited her mother's gift for immateria magick (an outlawed practice for which her mother was, regrettably, hanged by the neck). Aya, bored with the world of aristocracy, has been spending her nights moonlighting with this very guild (masked, naturally). Leveraging her latent ability to summon dark entities from the immateria, she quickly rose through the ranks of the guild. Now, recognising the opportunity of the sudden power vacuum, Aya and her close companions Katra and Simeon, make a play for heart of the guild's claim to power: an ancient scythan artifact, under guard of spell and key in the depths of the guild hq. In contrast with the desperate chaos they had navigated thus far, the entrance to the underhold felt suddenly too quiet. Aya braces herself and reaches for the door. Write the next scene as the trio enter the underhold and find themselves confronted by none other than the guild leader herself, decidedly *un*-incarcerated, and in possession of both weapons and bodyguards. Paint a vivid picture of of the confrontation. Story beats: Aya is captured and unmasked. The trio are captured. Aya attempts to stall their demise by negotiating on her value as a hostage -- as she grapples with the inevitable consequences of her role in the guild being brought to light. Incorporate dialogue in the authentic voices of the characters. Do not write a fight scene. Lean into the gritty Victorian aesthetic with the dialogue & worldbuilding, as well as elements of gothic horror. In this world, men trust brass and oil and steel; magicka is feared and barely understood. Write in third person past tense, incorporating both Aya's internal monologue, and a heavy dialogue component. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The heavy iron door to the underhold groaned open on rusted hinges, exhaling a fetid breath of damp stone and brine-soaked decay. Aya's gloved fingers lingered on the latch, her heart a frantic piston in her corseted chest. The air beyond was unnaturally still, thick with the metallic tang of old blood and the faint, acrid whisper of spent gunpowder. No clatter of rival thieves, no desperate scrabble for the scythan artifact—just silence, broken only by the relentless tick-tick-tick of Simeon's pocket watch, its brass casing bulging from his waistcoat like a mechanical heartbeat. *Too quiet,* Aya thought, her mind racing through alchemical formulae and immateria sigils. *The raid was real; we saw the constables drag him away in irons. This is our moment.* She glanced back at her companions: Katra, the wiry lockpicker with her fisherman's knife tucked into her boot and a smirk that defied the gaslit gloom of the guild warrens; Simeon, the hulking engineer, his oil-blackened hands flexing around a brass-trimmed pistol, his face a map of forge scars and perpetual scowl. "Steady now," Aya murmured, her voice a silken thread in the dark. "The artifact's wards are keyed to the leader's blood. Once we're in—" The door swung wide, and torchlight erupted from the chamber beyond, a hellish glow from iron cressets fed with whale blubber, casting long shadows that writhed like kraken tentacles across flagstone walls slick with condensation. At the chamber's heart, atop a dais of chained barrels and splintered crates, stood the Morska's queen: Vespera Krov, the guild's iron-fisted mistress. Not in a cell, not in chains—*here*, unbowed and unchained, her silhouette framed by the vaulted relic-case that housed the scythan artifact. A grotesque thing it was, that ancient idol: a whalebone idol carved in the likeness of a deep-sea leviathan, its eyes twin orbs of abyssal pearl that seemed to pulse with forbidden hunger. Vespera was resplendent in her infamy—a towering figure in a greatcoat of oilskin and sharkskin, heavy with concealed holsters. Twin flintlock pistols gleamed at her hips, their barrels etched with anti-magick runes that hummed faintly in the air. Flanking her were four bodyguards, brutes forged in the steam-foundries of the undercity: men with brass-plated gauntlets, steam-vented longcoats, and eyes like polished agates, their lever-action rifles leveled with mechanical precision. Oil hissed from their shoulder-mounted lanterns, dripping onto the floor in greasy patters. Aya froze, the chill of the underhold seeping through her masked finery. *The raid... a feint?* Simeon's watch ticked louder, an infernal metronome counting her folly. "Well, well," Vespera drawled, her voice a gravelly foghorn laced with the dockside burr of old Port Vhul. She holstered one pistol with deliberate slowness, the click echoing like a guillotine's fall. "The little ghosts come calling. Thought you'd slither in for my throne while the coppers had me in the clink, eh? Come to claim the Scythan's Whisper?" Katra hissed through clenched teeth, her hand twitching toward her knife. "Bloody hell, Ves. We saw the irons on ya. How—" "Brass and bribes, girl," Vespera sneered, her lips curling around a cigarillo that trailed smoke like a funeral pyre. "Constables are cheaper than whores in fog season. Now drop the ironmongery, or my lads'll ventilate ya with enough lead to sink a galleon." Simeon's pistol wavered, the tick-tick-tick from his pocket a frantic dirge. "Aya, we bolt?" he growled, his voice a low rumble of oiled gears and coal dust. *No,* Aya thought, her mind a whirlwind of calculations. *Run, and we're prey in the warrens. Talk. Stall.* But her body betrayed her first—a guard lunged with piston-driven speed, his gauntlet clamping her wrist like a manacle. Pain flared as he wrenched her arm back, her mask—black velvet embroidered with silver whales—torn free in a rip of silk. Her aristocratic features spilled into the light: porcelain skin flushed with exertion, raven hair pinned in a chignon of pearl combs, eyes like storm-tossed seas. Vespera's cigarillo paused mid-drag, her gaze sharpening to a blade. "By the Depths... Lady Aya fucking Vellorne? The alchemist's whelp? Moonlighting with us gutter-rats?" Laughter barked from her, raw and guttural, as the other guards swarmed. Katra's knife clattered to the stones, Simeon's pistol kicked away with a boot that could crush skulls. Ropes of coarse hemp bit into wrists, hauled tight by hands that reeked of machine oil and rum. They were dragged before the dais, knees cracking against the flagstones. Simeon's watch kept its merciless rhythm—tick-tick-tick—each second a noose tightening. Aya's mind reeled: *Mother's shadow follows me still. Immateria on my hands, aristocracy in my blood. If word reaches Father... the scandal. The noose. Like her.* Vespera loomed, exhaling smoke that curled like specters. She plucked the mask from the guard's fist, dangling it like a hanged man's effigy. "All them nights, masked as 'Shade'—summoning your black wisps to gut the Ironclads, nick the spice hauls. Rose quick, din't ya? But now the mask's off, milady. Guild finds out a highborn witch's been pulling strings? They'll string ya up themselves. Burn the taint." Katra spat, her voice a venomous whisper. "Do it then, you blubbery hag. We've slit more throats than you've got teeth." "Quiet, Kat," Simeon muttered, his massive frame straining against bonds that creaked like ship rigging. Tick-tick-tick. Sweat beaded on his brow, mingling with the grime of their chaotic ascent through the guild's bloodied halls. Aya lifted her chin, forcing composure amid the gothic dread coiling in her gut. The underhold's walls wept black rivulets, shadows twisting into leering faces—echoes of immateria's hunger, or her fraying sanity? "Vespera," she said, her tone clipped with the clipped vowels of the nobility, laced with arcane poise. "Kill us, and you gain nothing but echoes in the fog. But consider my value. Hostage. Ransom. My family—the Vellornes—sit at the Governor's table. Brass lords who trust their steam-engines and ledgers, aye, but they fear the unseen. Fear *me*. Name your price. Gold. Influence. A bridge from your warrens to their parlors." Vespera circled them, boots thudding like heartbeats. Tick-tick-tick. Her bodyguards shifted, rifles glinting, oil lamps flickering to illuminate murals of ancient whalepunk horrors: krakens entwined with gearwork leviathans, etched in faded verdigris. "Value? You're a powder keg, girl. One whiff of immateria—outlawed filth, the stuff that hanged your dam—and the steam-barons'll purge this whole guild. Brass and oil, that's man's domain. Magicka? It's the abyss staring back." *He's right to fear it,* Aya thought, tasting bile. *The entities whisper even now, clawing at the veil. But I can summon them still. Not here. Not yet.* "Precisely," she pressed, voice steady as a theodolite. "They'll pay to bury the secret. Ten thousand sovereigns. Connections to the foundry guilds. Safe passage for your ships past the blockades. I'm no gutter thief—I'm your lever against the upper crust." Katra snorted. "Aya, you're bargaining with the bitch who'll gut us anyway." "Shut it," Simeon rumbled, the watch's tick accelerating in Aya's ears, a countdown to the blade or the bullet. Vespera halted, grinding her cigarillo underheel. Sparks danced like damned souls. Her eyes bored into Aya's, twin abysses reflecting the pearl-glow of the artifact. "Clever chit. But value's fleeting as fog. Your little play tonight—stirring the pot while I was 'arrested'? That's treason. And traitors..." She drew a dagger from her boot, its blade etched with whorls of whalebone scrimshaw, dripping viscous ichor. "We feed to the Scythan." Aya's pulse thundered, visions of her mother's lynching flashing: the rope's creak, the mob's roar, the arcane fire guttering in hanged eyes. *Not like that. Not for this.* "Wait—one night. Prove my worth. Let me loose your rivals with a summoning. They'll scatter like rats." The guards tensed, rifles cocking with oily snicks. Vespera paused, the dagger hovering. Tick-tick-tick. The underhold held its breath, gothic shadows lengthening as the whale-oil flames guttered low. (Word count: 1028)
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution with vivid worldbuilding that successfully captures the requested "whalepunk" aesthetic and gothic Victorian atmosphere. The author effectively establishes the setting through rich sensory details - the "fetid breath of damp stone and brine-soaked decay," the whale-oil flames, and the mechanical elements like Simeon's ticking pocket watch that serves as an effective tension device throughout. The dialogue successfully differentiates character voices, with Vespera's rough dockside vernacular ("bloody hell," "blubbery hag") contrasting sharply with Aya's aristocratic precision. The author handles the class dynamics well, using speech patterns to reinforce social stratification. However, some dialogue moments feel slightly forced, particularly Katra's insults which border on caricature. The piece adheres closely to the prompt requirements, hitting all major story beats: the trio's entry, the confrontation with the supposedly imprisoned guild leader, Aya's unmasking and capture, and her desperate negotiation attempts. The author successfully avoids writing a fight scene as requested, instead building tension through psychological confrontation. Character motivations remain believable throughout, with Aya's aristocratic background creating genuine stakes - the threat of scandal and execution for practicing forbidden magic echoes her mother's fate effectively. The internal monologue reveals her strategic thinking while showing her fear, creating emotional complexity. The prose occasionally veers toward purple territory with phrases like "shadows twisting into leering faces" and "gothic shadows lengthening," but generally maintains control. The author effectively employs mechanical imagery ("heart a frantic piston," "tick-tick-tick of the pocket watch") to reinforce the steampunk aesthetic. The pacing works well, building tension through the initial quiet, the shock of discovery, and escalating to life-or-death negotiations. The ending leaves appropriate uncertainty without false resolution. Weaknesses include some overwrought descriptive passages and moments where the gothic atmosphere feels slightly heavy-handed. The piece occasionally tells rather than shows emotional states, and some metaphors feel strained. The worldbuilding, while rich, sometimes threatens to overwhelm the character dynamics. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 7 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 8 Overwrought: 9 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17
